data.title: Hovnanian Enterprises: The House Fell Down
data.description:
December 4th was the kind of day that I both love and hate. The reason why I hated it was because shares of a sizable holding of mine, homebuilder Hovnanian Enterprises (HOV), plummeted 22.5%. That took a nice chunk out of my portfolio. But this also relates to what I loved about the day. And that is the fact that I was able to jump in and buy even more of the stock as it dropped. It was down around 18% when I pulled that trigger. But I digressed.

This move lower by the company was driven by financial performance that, on the bottom line, fell short of analysts’ expectations. On top of this, management provided rather weak guidance when it comes to the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. Add on top of this broader concerns regarding the industry, and I can understand why some investors were scared. But as things stand, this is a top-notch player in the market, and shares are incredibly cheap relative to cash flows. So I have absolutely no problem keeping it rated a ‘strong buy’ for now, especially as management continues to make changes that should create additional value for investors in the long run.

The house came down

If we focus entirely on top-line results, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was actually pretty good for shareholders of Hovnanian Enterprises. The company reported revenue of $817.9 million. Admittedly, that marked a steep decline compared to the $979.6 million the business reported just one year earlier. However, the revenue that the company came out with exceeded analysts’ expectations by $3.4 million.

The overall drop in revenue for the company was driven by two primary factors. First and foremost, the number of homes delivered dropped from 1,982 to 1,811. And then on top of this, the average price of a home sold fell from $539,454 to $533,957. Considering that the housing market typically rises year after year, you might think this is peculiar. But when you read into what has gone on in this space over the last few years, it all makes sense.

Despite the fact that the population of the US continues to grow, there has become a big problem in the housing market in recent years. The COVID-19 pandemic sent prices skyrocketing because of supply chain issues that pushed input costs higher. Then, to add salt to the wound, the Federal Reserve had to hike interest rates in order to combat inflationary pressures. The end result here has been a massive affordability gap. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean.

Between the time that the housing market collapsed in 2008 and late 2021, there really was not an affordability gap in the housing market. The actual median income at the household level was enough to buy the median home. This affordability is determined through what is called the ‘qualified income’ measure. In essence, qualified income is the income needed in order to achieve homeownership costs that are equal to no more than 30% of annual income. But today, that gap is wide, even though it is coming down some. The median household income in the US stands at $84,834. But the qualified income for a $398,967 house is $121,411. This means that, unless you bought a home prior to 2022, there is a good chance you cannot reasonably afford one if you are an average American.

For a lot of homebuilders, this proved to be a boon. They were able to charge more and rake in more money during those boom years. But now, with economic conditions weakening, the demand for new houses happens to be dropping. As the chart above illustrates, the median sales price of a home in the US has been declining for a couple of years now. Furthermore, the economic climate is deteriorating because of tariffs and the fact that interest rates have had to remain high because of them. Naturally, the number of homes being built has dropped as a result, as the chart below shows.

In the first chart in this article, you can see financial results for all of 2025 compared to all of 2024. Although the picture was only particularly bad in the final quarter of this year when it comes to the top line, bottom-line results have been downright awful. For the final quarter of the year specifically, the company generated a loss per share of $0.51. That compares to the $12.79 per share profit reported a year ago. And it meant a decline in net profits from $91.7 million to negative $3.3 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $1.14 lower than what analysts were hoping to see. And unfortunately, as earnings declined, so too did EBITDA. I would normally like to look at operating cash flow also. But as of this writing, management has not come out with that data.

There are some other important metrics that we should take into consideration here. Most importantly, backlog for the company contracted sharply year over year, declining from 2,052 homes to 1,517 homes. At least we have seen a slight increase in the average price of a home in backlog, as the chart above illustrates. On top of this, net new contracts have been on the decline, with orders for 1,450 homes coming in during the most recent quarter compared to the 1,571 homes reported the previous year. Somehow, management has been able to increase the average price of a home ordered during this time. But some of that is likely due to tariffs, with some estimates putting the price increase on the average home built caused by tariffs at $8,900. If there is any good news here, besides the increase in the average price of a home in both backlog and homes ordered, is the fact that the cancellation rate for the company has actually come down slightly. In the final quarter of 2024, it was 18%. But this year, it was 17%. So on that front, at least the company seems to be holding up nicely.

There are a couple of other positive things that we need to take into consideration here. First and foremost, management has been successful in reducing overall debt for the company. In fact, total debt has dropped by $754 million from the end of 2019 through the final quarter of this year. In addition to this, management has been successful in restructuring its debt maturities. As the image below illustrates, debt did increase from $855 million at the end of July this year to $925 million at the end of October. This is, in and of itself, a bad thing. But simultaneous to this, the company was able to push maturities out years, and has been able to reduce annual interest expense from $87 million to $75 million. Management is very obviously working hard to improve the long-term outlook for the business.

In addition to making these types of changes, the company is also focused on some other initiatives. In fact, during their fourth quarter earnings call, the company reiterated its strategic focus on transit things, from acquiring new land to acquiring land that has higher ‘embedded incentives.’ This is in direct response to weak market conditions and is essentially a strategy of buying up lots that make it economical for the company to provide certain incentives for buyers, with examples being mortgage rate buydowns, price reductions, and various other promotional incentives. The goal here is to ultimately protect margins and potentially even boost them during these difficult times.

On top of this, the company continues to focus on cost-cutting to the extent that it can. However, it is worth noting that the near-term outlook is disappointing, which likely explains why shares fell so much. Management mentioned that for the first quarter of 2025, they expect to generate revenue of between $550 million and $650 million. For context, during the first quarter of 2025, revenue for the company was $673.6 million. But even more problematic is EBITDA. Management expects this to come in at between $35 million and $45 million for the quarter. But for 2025, it totaled $88.6 million.

If we use the results that the company achieved for the last two completed fiscal years now, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are valued. Even with the decline in profitability that it saw in 2025, the business is trading at the mid-single-digit range. In that same chart, you can see how it stacks up against five other similar enterprises. Without exception, our candidate is the cheapest of the group. But that's not the only way to value the business. In the chart below, I compared it to those same five firms on a price-to-book basis. And in this respect, it was around the middle of the group, with three of the businesses trading lower than it already is.

One critique that I have of the company is that I would like to see its net debt fall further. It's certainly not at a high level, especially compared to what it was a few years back. But it is at a level that I would like to see drop. In the chart below, you can see its net leverage ratio compared to these same five businesses. If market conditions improve, it would actually see its net leverage ratio drop rather substantially. But as things stand, two of the five homebuilders that I stacked it up against have less leverage than it does.

Takeaway

All things considered, I believe that Hovnanian Enterprises is one of the best opportunities on the market. Management has demonstrated that they know what they are doing. Of course, recent performance and the expectation of additional weakening in light of a challenging market are things that I don't like seeing. However, I view this as a buying opportunity. I was able to increase my exposure to the business during this time, and I firmly believe that will end up playing out well once the housing market starts to rebound. Because of this, I don't mind keeping the company rated a ‘strong buy.’

data.title: Blackstone Mortgage Trust: Improving Outlook For 2026
data.description:
Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) continued to shrink its impaired loan portfolio in the third quarter and managed to support its dividend with earnings available for distribution. Blackstone Mortgage Trust successfully stabilized its portfolio and income situation, and the REIT reported a decrease in its current expected credit loss reserve, which lowers investment risks for investors. The commercial mortgage REIT is not out of the woods yet in terms of its coverage profile, but it appears that last year's dividend cut really improved the REIT's financial situation. While I maintain a hold rating for now, I believe 2026 could potentially be a turnaround year for Blackstone Mortgage Trust if current earnings trends extrapolate.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Blackstone Mortgage a hold in my last work after the REIT’s second-quarter earnings due to the investment firm facing continual coverage challenges. Blackstone Mortgage is seeing an improving portfolio and coverage trend, indicating that the REIT is facing less pressure on its income, which obviously makes the dividend less risky. In my opinion, Blackstone Mortgage Trust could be set for a period of stronger book value growth in FY 2026 if the REIT further lowers its impaired loans and originates loans in other property types than offices.

Reduction in impaired loan portfolio, improving coverage trend

Blackstone Mortgage Trust made progress in reducing the size of its impaired loans on its balance sheet, which have been a considerable drag on the REIT’s financial metrics (such as distributable, earnings, loan losses) in the last two years.

As of the end of the September quarter, Blackstone Mortgage had $700M in impaired loans on its balance sheet, but this volume was down from $2.3B in the year-earlier period, showing a total decline rate of 71% year-over-year. As Blackstone Mortgage Trust lowers the amount of non-performing loans (mainly through asset sales), the CRE finance REIT is high-grading the risk profile of its balance sheet and exposing investors to fewer loan risks in the future.

In the most recent quarter, Blackstone Secured Lending generated $98.9M in income from loans and other interest-carrying investments, showing a decline rate of 9% Y/Y.

Blackstone Mortgage Trust has sold loans and assets, some below book value, in order to de-risk its balance earlier this year. These strategic actions have reduced the REIT's reliance on interest income from loans… the majority of which are still related to offices. Blackstone Mortgage Trust had approximately 29% office exposure in the September quarter, according to its collateral breakdown in Q3, showing a 4 PP contraction compared to the year-earlier period.

As a result, the REIT's reliance on income from loans also decreased, while income from ancillary sources has become more prevalent. In fact, the decline in income from loans (-9% Y/Y) was more than compensated for by other revenue sources (+838%), like owned real estate, which caused BXMT to actually report positive (+19% Y/Y) revenue growth.

My expectation here for Blackstone Mortgage, especially as we head into a lower interest-rate world in 2026, is that the REIT will strategically shift reinvestment proceeds from loan pay-offs/early repayments into non-loan segments, like owned real estate. Within the loan segment, I can see BXMT shift more funds into non-office property types, such as industrial, which is seeing strong demand amid growing investments in e-Commerce infrastructure. I expect the market to see at least three rate cuts next year, which could further BXMT's shift away from short-term, variable-rate mortgage loans in FY 2026.

Blackstone Mortgage reported distributable earnings/EAD of $0.48 per share in Q3'25 which was the second consecutive quarter of EAD growth for the CRE finance REIT. Like Ladder Capital (LADR), Blackstone Mortgage Trust showed an improving trend in core earnings and, correspondingly, better dividend coverage.

In terms of coverage, Blackstone Mortgage again fully earned its dividend with distributable earnings in the September quarter, and the CRE finance REIT did report a decline in it CECL reserve, highlighting improving asset quality: BXMT's CECL reserve declined $1M Q/Q to $696M.

Because of a reduction in impaired loans, Blackstone Mortgage REIT had the second-best coverage ratio (102%) after Ladder Capital (109%). The worst was Starwood Property (STWD) with 83% coverage. Starwood Property has a lot of access to liquidity, however, and has a more diversified portfolio (it owns mortgage securities and has a servicing business, plus owned real estate), which gives the REIT optionality in defending its dividend, despite under-earning it.

It seems to me that Blackstone Mortgage Trust’s 24% dividend cut last year clearly has benefited the REIT and led to the successive restoration of its dividend coverage profile. If this trend continues in 2026 and BXMT achieves a full rolling year of 100%+ coverage, I would be willing to update by rating from hold to buy.

I am sticking with a hold rating for now...

Shares of commercial mortgage REITs have begun to trade at discounts to book value because of investor concerns about interest rates, loan quality, and dividend sustainability. Shares of Blackstone Mortgage are currently trading at an 8% discount to book value, which compares to a 6% discount for Ladder Capital (LADR) and a 1% discount for Starwood Property Trust.

Blackstone Mortgage Trust currently has the highest P/E ratio of 14.1X on a forward basis, but as the REIT recycles more of its loan payoffs into other income-generating assets in non-CRE loan categories and hopefully avoids more loan losses going forward as a result, it should be able to return to a period of strong earnings and book value growth.

Starwood Property Trust until recently was priced at a premium to book value, but this premium disappeared after the REIT reported its third consecutive quarter of EAD falling below its dividend: Not Out Of The Woods Just Yet. My rating on STWD is hold.

Ladder Capital is my favorite CRE finance REIT because it did not cut its dividend and, as of right now, has the highest excess dividend coverage. My rating on LADR is strong buy.

In the long run, I would expect Blackstone Mortgage Trust to revalue to book value, under the condition that the company won't have to add funds to its CECL reserve and the shift away from high-risk mortgage loans continues. As of the end of the September quarter, Blackstone Mortgage Trust had a book value per share of $20.99, which included $4.16 per share in CECL reserves.

Risks with Blackstone Mortgage

If the mortgage REIT continues to support its dividend with earnings available for distribution, I believe a dividend cut could be avoided. On the other hand, should losses pop up again in the office loan portfolio in FY 2026 and the REIT see a decline in its distributable earnings, another dividend cut may be on the table. What would change my mind specifically about BXMT is if 1) The REIT's dividend coverage ratio dropped below 100% for two consecutive quarters, 2) Impaired loans, origination from offices, rose again in Q4 or in FY 2026, and 3) The REIT failed to push for new loan originations in non-office CRE categories such as industrial or multi-family.

Final thoughts

Blackstone Mortgage’s portfolio performance has slightly improved compared to my last coverage, and the mortgage REIT supported its dividend with distributable earnings… which are two positive developments coming out of Blackstone Mortgage’s recent financial disclosures. A major change has occurred in the company's revenue mix, with BXMT now less dependent on mortgage interest income. I expect this trend to continue in 2026, which could signify a broader shift away from high-risk office mortgage loans. Industrial real estate, multi-family real estate, CRE securities, or owned real estate are all potential destinations for BXMT's capital. Further, with coverage trends improving, Blackstone Mortgage Trust is in a much healthier position, from a payout perspective, than Starwood Property Trust.

data.title: IDMO: A Potential Core Of A (Truly) Ex-U.S. Competitive Portfolio
data.description:
I have always appreciated momentum strategies. Together with the quality factor, I think they are two very virtuous approaches to the market, which could bring value to a portfolio, as a core or satellite component; and when we talk about the ex. US market, the first ETF that comes to my mind is the Invesco S&P International Developed Momentum ETF (IDMO).

The ETF seems to interface well with this market category, as can be deduced from the peer analysis. But the question I personally ask myself is: how could I make it interact best with a portfolio? Well, I have an opinion, simple yet distinctive.

But first …

What is IDMO?

The ETF replicates the S&P World Ex-U.S. Momentum Index, which seeks to represent developed market stocks excluding the U.S. and South Korea with the highest momentum. The participation cost is given by the expense ratio of 0.25%, which, however, is offset by a 30-day SEC yield of 2.26%, which I personally appreciate a lot.

Since March 18, 2016, the benchmark has changed. Before, the index was the S&P BMI International Developed High Beta Index. The report confirms that the long-term performance incorporates the old High Beta index until 2016. With the switch, an additional piece is introduced, which is essentially the introduction of a clearer momentum filter. But let’s look at it more closely.

Strategy

I want to give a quick excursus of the index strategy, because it is somewhat the heart of the ETF. In this sense, the prospectus finally clarifies the complete process:

  1. The index looks at the 12-month performance, excluding the last month (to avoid short-term mean reversion).
  2. Then there is an adjustment for volatility, which penalizes highly volatile stocks.
  3. A descending ranking of the scores is done.
  4. And then there is the inclusion of the top 20%, weighted through modified market-cap weighting.

This process leads to a distinctive result.

Holding breakdown

The result is about 181 holdings, an element that gives an important diversifying touch for a momentum strategist ETF.

Being a global ex. US index, the distribution of the holdings is geographically varied, which can be summarized as follows:

But the truly interesting thing is the sector distribution, composed as follows:

And it is interesting because almost 50% of the portfolio is in financials, which is an anomaly compared to classic global ETFs, where info. tech is still the dominant sector. This has direct impacts on the aggregated fundamentals: the fund has a P/E of 17x and a fwd. of 14x, noticeably lower compared simply to the S&P 500, while still maintaining a competitive ROE close to 15%, which still denotes a certain quality of the composition.

To understand better, it is necessary to evaluate the peers.

The concentration on financials

The hyper-concentration toward financials is a distinctive element of IDMO today: this has given it strong momentum over the last 3Y (+84.13%), but at the same time it also creates a lot of uncertainty.

Specifically, I am referring to the future trend of interest rates: with rising rates, banks benefit from higher NIM. With falling rates, NIM “could” contract. Considering that monetary policies of ex-US central banks have been more accommodative, this is an element to discuss.

Now, we can make 3 additional considerations in this direction:

  • The first is that NIM does not depend only on central bank policy, considering that this influences short-term rates. The curves are in bear steepening, therefore, extra-US banking activity continues today, in my opinion, to still have competitive margins.
  • Then, secondly, the fact that it is not only NIM that influences banking activity, especially if the financial sector in question is not a commercial banking activity. Financial activity is branched, moving from the insurance segment to wealth management: a contraction in rates in this sense favors a normalization of reserves, expands banking activity, and stimulates financial markets.
  • The third point to reason about is the momentum nature of the ETF. If the evolution of the money market were to lead to a repricing of financial assets, these would lose momentum and therefore their predominant position within the ETF. And this would not be unrealistic: I want to remind you that IDMO has a turnover above 100%, an element that clearly explains a very high semiannual portfolio reorganization activity.

Peer comparison

I start with a first parallel with a world ex. US equity ETF, in this case EFA (which also excludes Canada). It is interesting to note how, despite the 2022 drawdowns being common, and the subsequent recovery being similar, in the following ex. US expansionary phase of the cycle, the momentum strategy managed to clearly detach itself from the classic EAFE index. The same comparison could be made with other variants, such as ACWX, and the result would not change.

Having acknowledged this consideration: how could it be included in a portfolio? Other comparisons are needed.

First of all, it cannot be a substitute for the S&P 500, considering an R² = 0.69, a beta below 1, and a negative alpha. And therefore it cannot simply be substituted for classic MSCI World ETFs either, because the US component, which represents about 70% of the index distribution (important and of strong added value), would be missing.

A competitive solution

If we rely on the logic according to which the market will remain well represented by the momentum factor in the future as well, we can move in two directions.

  • Either set up an equity component composed of 70% SPY and 30% IDMO. And already here, from the backtest, you can see a more competitive Sharpe ratio than the classic URTH (equal to 0.83 vs. URTH’s 0.72). The reason is slightly lower volatility and drawdowns, and a CAGR almost 2% higher.

  • Or manage volatility: a 100% momentum portfolio, where the core component that is at 70%, is covered by SPMO, while the 30% comes from IDMO. The result is even more pronounced in favor of the sample portfolio, with a Sharpe ratio of 0.94. An annualized return CAGR from 2016 of 16% compared to URTH’s roughly 12%.

Now we could counter: “Naturally, the risk is an increase in volatility and drawdowns!”

In reality, “kind of”. When we use the combination of SPY and IDMO, there are no such marked deviations in terms of volatility and max drawdown. Instead, when we move to SPMO and IDMO (a total momentum portfolio), there is interesting risk-related evidence. It is true, volatility increases, but the max drawdown in the reference period actually decreases: URTH has a max drawdown of 25.48%, while the sample portfolio has 22.34%.

Risk

So far we have addressed the issue of high turnover as a positive element of adaptation and as a core feature of a strategy used by IDMO to outperform its reference segment.

It is, however, appropriate to remember that this also makes IDMO’s expected performance difficult to predict. If for a classic passive ETF, which, for example, has a market-weight holding distribution, one can carry out valuation analyses with fundamental metrics such as the classic P/E to estimate a fair value; for an ETF like IDMO with such a high semiannual turnover, this becomes practically impossible.

What an investor is practically forced to do is trust that the momentum strategy will also in the future manage to represent ex-US markets well. And in my opinion, this is a form of risk in IDMO not to be underestimated. In fact, it can lead in the short term to:

  • greater drawdowns (even though we have not seen this happen in recent history): especially in moments of sudden reversal, when the sectors with the highest momentum, if overvalued, experience a forced mean reversion.

  • potential underperformance in two cases: the first if the momentum strategy stops representing the market well; the second in moments of intra-semester bull reversion, where, for example, after a bear market, a catalyst emerges that reverses the market, like AI in 2022–2023. IDMO, being positioned on defensive segments because they carry higher momentum, would not benefit from the change.

Conclusion

Personally, I consider IDMO a good tool to participate in the market; it remains a personal opinion, but its momentum nature seems to have managed to give an extra boost to performance, an element that I personally find common also in other momentum ETFs.

data.title: SMDV: Low Valuation And Volatility, Weak Performance
data.description:
The Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) comes as an option for income-oriented investors in the small-cap category. It has a defensive tilt, which is interesting, especially within small caps, where volatility is typically more elevated than in the broader market. The next sections will detail the fund’s main characteristics, breaking down its core holdings and evaluating key metrics, such as valuation, performance, and tradability relative to the Russell 2000 and other dividend funds in the segment.

ETF Description & Highlights

SMDV follows a rules-based approach, tracking the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index, designed by FTSE, which selects at least 40 small-cap companies from the parent Russell 2000 Index with a long track record of consistent dividend increases. Companies that constitute the index are equally weighted, subject to quarterly rebalances to adjust for price fluctuations, and a sector cap that prevents a single sector from exceeding 30% of the index.

As of November 26, 2025, SMDV had $628.8 million in assets, invested across 106 companies, with an average market capitalization of $2.10 billion, which highlights the fund’s strong tilt toward the lower end of the market capitalization spectrum. For comparison, the Russell 2000’s average market cap sits at nearly $3 billion, 50% higher than SMDV.

Due to SMDV’s equal-weighted structure, the fund has a fairly diluted stock composition, with the top ten holdings representing only 12% of the fund. The largest holdings currently are mostly utilities, reflecting short-term outperformance after the latest rebalances. This list includes Black Hills (1.18%), Portland General Electric (1.15%), NorthWestern Energy Group (1.14%), Northwest Natural Holding (1.14%), and Spire (1.13%). Other top ten holdings are Hillenbrand (1.17%), Standex International (1.12%), CSG Systems International (1.17%), Andersons (1.20%), and HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (1.16%), blending companies from industrials, technology, staples, and financial services, but with none of them accounting for more than 1.20% of the fund.

The following table presents a list of small- and mid-cap ETFs with a primary focus on dividend strategies that will serve as the peer group for SMDV in this analysis. This group includes funds that add slight strategy variations, spanning quality dividend and dividend growth, but still keeping dividend exposure as the core investment theme.

The first is the First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (SDVY), which employs a rules-based framework that filters for companies in the small- and mid-cap space with improving dividends and solid fundamentals, using an equal-weight methodology, similar to SMDV. Next, the WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend ETF (DON) weights constituents according to their cash dividends. Meanwhile, the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend ETF (DES) also applies a dividend-weighted philosophy with a focus on the small-cap segment. The ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL) is a mid-cap version of SMDV, which selects companies that have increased dividends for at least 15 years. Two other funds round out this group with quality-oriented approaches: ALPS O'Shares U.S. Small-Cap Quality Dividend ETF (OUSM) favors small-cap companies with healthy balance sheets, dividend sustainability, and lower volatility, while WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRS) screens for companies that pay dividends and have shown consistent earnings growth over time.

SMDV has a large exposure to financial services, which account for 31.3% of the fund. Together with industrials (20.9%) and utilities (18.7%), these three sectors form the core of the portfolio. A second tier includes real estate (7.8%) and basic materials (7.2%), which still account for a significant share of the fund. Further down the list, it holds modest positions in consumer staples (4.7%), consumer discretionary (3.3%), and technology (2.8%), with marginal allocations to healthcare (1.9%) and communication services (1.4%). Relative to the Russell 2000, SMDV is most overweight in utilities (+15.4%), financial services (+14.7%), areas with above-average dividend yields, and also industrials (+5.4%). On the other hand, the fund is significantly underweight in healthcare (-16.8%), as well as in technology (-12.7%) and consumer discretionary (-6.1%).

Let’s now break down SMDV’s allocation across its core sectors. In financial services, for example, the fund has an overwhelming allocation to banks, spread across more than 25 holdings, including names such as Community Bank Financial Institutions, regional lender Cadence Bank, mid-Atlantic player WesBanco, and Pennsylvania-based S&T Bancorp. In addition, the fund still has smaller exposure to insurers with holdings in regional carrier Donegal Group and workers’ compensation player AMERISAFE, while mortgage REIT HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital and the education niche insurer Horace Mann provide a bit of diversification in the sector.

In industrials, nearly half of the allocation is in industrial machinery with 12 holdings, such as industrial equipment supplier Hillenbrand, precision-engineered products manufacturer Standex International, and pump maker Gorman-Rupp. Other segments include commercial services (e.g., Matthews International, ABM Industries, and HNI), trading companies and distributors (e.g., GATX and McGrath Rent), while the exposure to building products comes from diversified manufacturer Griffon and glass specialist Apogee Enterprises.

The utilities sleeve, SMDV’s third-largest sector, also has a fragmented allocation, given its equal-weighted design, with 19 holdings in a well-balanced distribution across four segments: gas utilities, electric utilities, water utilities, and multi-utilities. The fund owns regional natural-gas distributors Northwest Natural, Spire, and Chesapeake Utilities, while electric utilities include Portland General Electric, diversified upper-Midwest provider ALLETE, and Texas-based TXNM Energy. Water utilities are represented by players like York Water, American States Water, and California Water Service, and multi-utilities complete the sector exposure through Black Hills, NorthWestern Energy Group, Avista, and Unitil, all of these combining electric and gas networks in smaller regional markets.

Outside the largest sectors, the fund’s remaining industries are spread across a range of sectors, helping keep the portfolio relatively well diversified. In materials, SMDV owns companies such as the specialty nutrition and ingredients producer Balchem, industrial and specialty chemicals maker Quaker Chemical, and adhesive manufacturer HB Fuller. Consumer discretionary exposure includes specialty retailers Sonic Automotive and Shoe Carnival, as well as leisure-products manufacturer Polaris. The fund also holds metals and mining name Materion, healthcare providers via senior-care operator Ensign Group, along with smaller positions in media, tobacco, packaged foods, and household products. In sum, this complementary allocation adds further breadth for the fund, offering exposure to cyclical areas without overshadowing the fund’s more defensive tilt.

SMDV shows a clear defensive bias compared to the peer group of dividend ETFs. The fund is overweight in utilities (+11.7%), driven by its large exposure to the sector, while peers like SDVY, OUSM, and DGRS only have marginal positions in utilities. The exception is REGL, which also holds nearly 18% in this segment. SMDV also has a greater exposure to financial services (+4.7%), driven by its 31.3% stance in the sector, though it is not the only one: SDVY and REGL also adopt an aggressive approach, with a similar 31% exposure. This creates a cluster of financials-heavy strategies (SMDV, SDVY, REGL), unlike other peers that keep a more diversified strategy.

On the flip side, SMDV is underweight in consumer discretionary (-12.2%), as the fund holds only 3.3% in the sector, while OUSM and DGRS keep nearly 20% exposure. As a result, SMDV keeps a quite high sector correlation with REGL (0.94), while it remains reasonably strong with SDVY (0.73), DON (0.83), and DES (0.82). Meanwhile, the fund ends up with only a modest correlation with OUSM (0.56) and DGRS (0.56), as these funds take a contrasting strategy with regard to their exposure to utility and consumer discretionary.

Better Than Average Dividend Yield And Growth

SMDV’s 2.6% dividend yield is the highest in the group, reflecting its heavy exposure to two of the highest-yielding sectors, financial services and utilities. The fund’s dividend growth has also been above average, particularly over the 3-year period, while funds like DON and DES have delivered an arguably weak dividend growth. Still, SMDV has lagged OUSM’s dividend growth, likely driven by OUSM’s heavier exposure to high-growth sectors, such as technology (12.1%) and consumer discretionary (19.7%)

Valuation Below The Russell 2000

SMDV’s 13.4x P/E represents a roughly 11% discount to the Russell 2000’s 15.1x. This gap is largely spread across several key sectors, especially financial services, industrials, and utilities, where SMDV holds oversized positions relative to the benchmark. The fund’s financial services sleeve trades at 11.7x P/E, below the benchmark’s 13.1x, while industrials carry a 20.4x multiple versus the Russell 2000’s 29.5x, creating one of the largest sector valuation gaps. The fund’s tilt toward utilities also drags valuation down, given the sector’s modest P/E of 16x. At the individual stock level, holdings in financial services are a key factor, as a large group of regional banks and insurers sit well below the benchmark’s P/E average. Names like Financial Institutions Inc. (7.9x), Orrstown (8.4x), and ChoiceOne (8.8x) illustrate the discounted valuations across the fund very well.

The fund shows a mixed growth profile. SMDV’s historical earnings growth (21.7%) far surpasses the Russell 2000’s -1.9%, but its sales growth of 2.0% falls short of the benchmark’s 3.0%. A meaningful support factor here is in financial services and industrials, where the fund’s sector 3-year EPS growth average is slightly higher than the benchmark. On the other hand, SMDV's weaker sales growth seems broad-based. Most sectors exhibit softer growth, with healthcare, industrials, and technology exceeding a 10 percentage point gap.

On profitability, SMDV actually looks healthier than the Russell 2000. The fund posts an EBITDA margin of 28.0%, versus 15.5% for the benchmark, and its ROE sits near 9.6%, while the index as a whole shows a slightly negative figure around -3.8%. This is helped by stronger margins in healthcare, industrials, real estate, and technology. In real estate, for instance, SMDV’s REIT holdings post an EBITDA margin near 69%, comfortably ahead of the benchmark’s 47%, thanks to high-margin names like Gladstone Land, CareTrust REIT, Getty Realty, and Universal Health Realty, all of which run EBITDA in the 50-80% range. Industrials and utilities also contribute, with sector margins around 19% and 37%, respectively, supported by companies like GATX, Exponent, York Water, and American States Water. At the same time, the fund’s low exposure to healthcare also plays a key role, as it has no holdings in unprofitable names from the Russell 2000 in the segment, such as BridgeBio Pharma, Guardant Health, and Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, among others.

SMDV’s P/E is just slightly above the peer group. DES and DGRS have the lowest valuations among peers, with P/E below 12x, thanks to their strong tilt toward small and micro caps, which typically drive valuation down. At the higher end of the valuation range, SDVY’s P/E multiple is influenced by its 27.6% exposure to mid caps. Still, all peers trade below the Russell 2000’s 15.1x P/E, reflecting an overall underweight allocation to high-growth sectors, such as technology and consumer discretionary, while maintaining a large exposure to value areas like financial services, utilities, and consumer staples to a lesser extent.

Underperforming The Russell 2000 And Peers

SMDV’s performance has been subdued. The fund’s annualized returns of 3.6% (3-year) and 6.7% (5-year) have lagged the Russell 2000 by 7.8 and 1.2 percentage points, respectively. The most significant underperformance versus the benchmark occurred in 2020 and 2023, when SMDV saw negative returns, and year-to-date, as the fund’s flat returns trailed the Russell 2000 by a large margin. The only bright spot came in 2022, given the fund’s relative resilience, declining only 5.9% compared with the Russell 2000’s -20.5% returns.

SMDV has been consistently the worst-performing fund compared with peers over the past years, with a few exceptions, like in 2022, when it held up better, as more aggressive peers like SDVY, DES, and DGRS dropped more than 10%.

The fund’s volatility helps explain much of its admittedly weak gains over the past years. With betas of 0.90 (3-year) and 0.83 (5-year), SMDV has seen much more contained price moves than the Russell 2000, where beta has been in the 1.09-1.30 range, as well as most peers, especially SDVY, DES, and DGRS, whose betas have been above 1.00 most of the time. But it is fair to say that peers like REGL and OUSM have also seen lower volatility than the broader market, with betas below 1.00. As a result, SMDV has seen modest price moves both to the downside and the upside. Its upside capture ratio in the 60-71 range shows limited participation during rallies, one of the lowest in the group, along with REGL. On the flip side, the fund’s downside capture ratio between 95 and 133 is mid-range relative to peers, but well above the Russell 2000, indicating a historical trend to suffer steeper declines on downturns. Overall, despite its conservative profile, SMDV has shown a sort of asymmetry, where it has delivered one of the weakest gains in risk-on environments, while not handily outperforming during declines.

With an average daily volume of about $1.51 million, SMDV is not actively traded like most peers, particularly SDVY, which sees close to $48 million a day. This leads to an average bid-ask spread of 0.09%, which is wider than the SDVY and DON, but in line with the peer group. The fund’s 38% turnover is slightly below peers’ average, while its 0.40% expense ratio is similar to most peers, only marginally lower than SDVY and OUSM. However, SMDV exhibits one of the highest tracking errors, along with DGRS, though the peer group as a whole shows above-average tracking error relative to the ETF market. Finally, SMDV’s Sharpe ratios of 0.01 (3-year) and 0.27 (5-year) are by far the lowest among peers, as relatively low volatility cannot offset weaker returns over the past few years.

Final Thoughts

Given SMDV’s focus on companies with a long history of raising dividends, it comes as no surprise that the fund’s portfolio leans into mature sectors, such as financials, utilities, and industrials. Profitability metrics are also strong, valuation is attractive relative to the Russell 2000, and the dividend yield is at the high end of the peer group. Sure, this is an income- and value-oriented investment with a low-beta profile in a market that continues to grind higher, led by growth stocks, particularly in technology, amid rate-cut expectations and a relatively benign slowing growth scenario.

With that in mind, SMDV appears better positioned for capital preservation, which may appeal to investors concerned about elevated valuations in large caps, especially if growth softens further. The flip side is the fund’s consistent underperformance over time, driven in large part by limited gains in risk-on environments, reflecting its very conservative profile. That said, performance aside, I believe that SMDV still has some appeal for investors looking for income and potential upside, given the fund’s attractive valuation as well as small caps in general. However, it is not among the best options for those seeking outperformance, due to its defensive tilt.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The S&P 500 Index increased by 8.12% (total return, in USD) in the third quarter of 2025, whereas the Russell 2000 Index increased by 12.40% (total return, in USD). The major US indices continued to notch repeated record highs this quarter as solid economic data, strong earnings growth, resilient consumer spending, and the Federal Open Market Committee’s commencement of a monetary easing cycle encouraged a bullish market sentiment. Optimism toward the artificial intelligence growth theme persisted during the period, as corporate earnings exceeded expectations and consumer spending remained strong, underscoring the potential longevity of the theme. Concurrently, weakening labor data contributed to the September rate cut, while mixed Fedspeak leaves the pace of additional easing through year-end uncertain. The best performing sectors within the S&P 500 were Information Technology, Communication Services, and Consumer Discretionary, while the worst performing sectors were Consumer Staples, Real Estate, and Materials. For the Russell 2000, the best performing sectors were Materials, Industrials, and Communication Services while the worst performing sectors were Consumer Staples, Financials, and Real Estate.

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs Mid Cap Value Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap Value Index (net), during the quarter. The Consumer Discretionary and Industrials sectors contributed to returns, while the Financials and Information Technology sectors detracted from returns.

Western Digital Corporation (WDC)(1.9%) was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company is a global provider of solutions for digital content collection, storage, management, protection, and use. Western Digital Corporation’s stock price soared following its robust earnings results, in which it exceeded expectations across the board and delivered a positive outlook. The company’s results were fueled by continued strong demand within its cloud segment, alongside increasing needs for artificial intelligence-driven data storage. We continue to believe Western Digital Corporation is an industry leader in the hard disk drive market, strategically positioned for structural growth driven by increasing cloud capital expenditure and the emergence of artificial intelligence with its sales increasingly dominated by data centers. We believe this strong market position, coupled with anticipated margin and free cash flow improvements, suggests significant upside potential.

D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI)(2.1%), a homebuilding company, was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company’s stock price rose on the back of its exceptionally strong financial performance and execution in its recent fiscal quarter. Despite a challenging housing market, D.R. Horton’s results exceeded market expectations, underscoring its operational resilience and strategic effectiveness. We continue to believe the company could benefit from industry consolidation tailwinds and a correction of the supply/demand imbalance in the housing market. D.R. Horton’s financial strength, demonstrated by robust free cash flow and share repurchases, reinforced our confidence in its ability to capitalize on the anticipated sector recovery. This optimism was further underpinned by the ongoing consolidation within the industry, where larger, more financially sound builders are increasing market share, creating a more steady and predictable competitive landscape.

The cloud-based project management software company, Monday.com LTD (MNDY) (0.0%), was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. Following its second quarter earnings report, the company’s stock price experienced a notable decline, despite exceeding analyst expectations for revenue and earnings per share. This downturn was primarily attributed to a deceleration in revenue growth compared to prior quarters and management’s acknowledgement of headwinds stemming from evolving consumer search habits, which have particularly impacted net new customer acquisition among smaller clients. While we continue to like and monitor the name, we ultimately exited our position in favor of better risk/reward for the portfolio at this time.

Our position in operator of value-focused membership warehouse clubs, BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (BJ)(1.7%) was a top detractor from relative returns during the third quarter. Despite beating estimated earnings per share and bottom-line results, the company’s stock fell this quarter due to a slight revenue miss and conservative forward guidance from the management team. The company called out a tough start to the quarter due to inclement weather reducing foot traffic, but sales trends sequentially improved through the quarter. Despite some lumpiness in performance, we believe the negative reaction may be oversold as we view the company as a resilient business, with a strong defensive position in the food retailing category, that remains at a modest valuation. As consumers across all income levels grow incrementally more cautious, BJ’s value-oriented strategy of protecting consumers from tariff pressures, may grow more appealing. Furthermore, despite slightly missing expectations, the company deliver its 14th straight quarter of traffic growth and 11th quarter of market share growth, demonstrating the company’s legacy of strong execution and stable growth. Overall, we remain convicted in the name as the company’s diversified revenue streams, resilient management of margins, and historic growth patterns make this company a potentially attractive opportunity in the long term.

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in radiation detection and monitoring solution provider, Mirion Technologies (MIR)(1.5%), during the quarter. Amidst the wave of broad-based artificial intelligence adoption, nuclear power demand has continued to grow, which we view as a prominent tailwind for Mirion Technologies. As the company provides safety solutions for the nuclear and defense industries, we believe its favorable operating environment positioning combined with its financial health and deft management team may make it an attractive investment among peers.

We initiated a position in homebuilding materials manufacturer and distributor, Builders FirstSource, Inc. (BLDR)(1.3%), during the quarter. We believe Builders FirstSource is a highly capable operator demonstrating strong operational resilience and profitability despite a challenging housing market, largely due to its strategic shift towards higher margin value-added products. As a leading player in single-family home construction, we are confident in Builders FirstSource long-term and believe the company is well-positioned to continue to deliver growth as single-family housing recovers. The company’s strong free cash flow generation, history of significant share buybacks, and historical outperformance coming out of a housing downturn suggest compelling long-term earnings upside.

We exited our position in the cloud-based project management software company, Monday.com LTD (0.0%), during the quarter. Despite exceeding analyst expectations for revenue and earnings per share in its second quarter earnings report, the company reported a deceleration in revenue growth compared to prior quarters. Furthermore, management acknowledged headwinds stemming from evolving consumer search habits, which have particularly impacted net new customer acquisition among smaller clients. While we continue to like and monitor the name, we ultimately exited our position in favor of better risk/reward for the portfolio at this time.

We exited our position in regional bank holding company, M&T Bank Corporation (MTB)(0.0%), during the quarter. While we continue to like and monitor the name, we ultimately decided to exit our position as we viewed other regional banks as more favorable from both a risk/reward and future growth opportunity standpoint. Specifically, our long-term conviction waned as the company reduced its future guidance for net interest income due to continued softness in commercial growth.

Strategy/Outlook

The third quarter demonstrated broadening market strength, underpinned by renewed leadership of artificial intelligence (AI) excitement, the commencement of a monetary easing cycle, and prominent themes of deregulation. Given the uncertain pacing of further interest rate reductions, softening labor market dynamics, and lofty stock valuations, we believe active management may become critical in identifying quality names and capitalizing on the dispersing outperformance as AI adoption extends beyond traditional technology enablers and beneficiaries. Unlike passive management strategies, which may carry exposure to low-quality constituents, taking an active approach may enable investors to avoid potential pitfalls and adapt to shifting macroeconomic conditions and competitive landscape. As we navigate this period of heightened volatility, we remain nimble and look to capitalize on idiosyncratic opportunities uncovered through bottom-up stock selection. We continue to prioritize our quality-oriented approach to investing by focusing on having a long-term viewpoint on the portfolio, seeking businesses with healthy balance sheets, and partnering with management teams that are effective stewards of capital. In our view, we are optimistic that a fundamental approach may generate excess returns in the long run for our clients.
